reasoning: The informal statement says 8 is the smallest positive integer satisfying n ≡ 2 (mod 3), n ≡ 3 (mod 5), n ≡ 1 (mod 7). The Lean theorem asserts: (1) a positive-integer solution exists, (2) every positive-integer solution is ≥ 8, and (3) 8 itself satisfies all three congruences. Parts (2) and (3) together exactly say "8 is the least positive integer satisfying the system," and part (1) is a redundant-but-true consequence of (3). The congruence predicates correctly encode n % k = r, and using ℕ+ correctly captures "positive integer." So the Lean signature is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
